fact: September 16 sits exactly one week before the autumn equinox, when day and night become perfectly equal twins!
In the Northern Hemisphere, this is prime spider season - house spiders are frantically searching for mates and cozy winter hideouts in your home!
Maple trees in cold regions are just starting their color-changing magic show, with sugar rushing from leaves back to roots.
Why do sunsets look extra orange around now? The sun sits lower in the sky, so its light travels through more atmosphere, filtering out blues and leaving fiery colors.
September 16 marks day 259 of the year - that means only 106 days left until New Year's Eve!
Monarch butterflies in North America are in full migration mode, with some flying over 2,000 miles to Mexico without ever making the return trip.
In Australia and other Southern Hemisphere places, this date brings the sweet smell of spring flowers blooming after winter's end.
Squirrels across North America enter their busiest hoarding phase, burying up to 10,000 nuts each to survive winter.
September 16 falls smack in the middle of Virgo season, making it one of the most 'Virgo-ish' days of the entire year.
In northern regions, the first frost warnings start appearing around this time, as nighttime temperatures begin their chilly dive toward winter.
Daylight shrinks by about 3 minutes each day in mid-September - that's 21 minutes less sunlight than just one week earlier!
